item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you, bungo peef, for your insightful commentary

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ketamine.

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded:
The sheer size of your rage-rumbling is of equal proportions to this SCP.

I have a mental disorder. I didn't writ an article about it, I need no sympathy and I don't think this as a particularly good SCP either, although I do find it lightly appealing, so I upvoted.

What was the need for such a lengthy text just to say you don't like that people like this SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is more like a tale, doesn't mean it's bad, +1 anyways, I like different things.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Author has confirmed ownership of the images in private with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could read this for the first time again. I could literally feel this article. Phenomenal job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't just my favorite SCP, this is my favorite fictional writing of all time. And I can't really explain why.

But I guess I'll try
– hide block

I'll be completely honest, I tried to dislike this. "Ah, it's just random scribblings. The author didn't even try to edit it down. It's too blatant, it's too mysterious."

But that's the thing, the particulars really don't matter to me here. You could get rid of a few lines, you could add a few. You could move the Only section, or change their number. You could completely rewire it from scratch, leaving the important bits near the end, and it wouldn't change a whole lot.

It's the whole scope of it. The feeling it gives me while reading. This article crawled out of the screen and hopped on my shoulder and read itself with me. Then, as we got deeper, it wriggled its way into my ear, to my brain, and sat behind my eyes, reading itself. Then, by the end, it was just the article. Reading itself. Like 426, but existential, meaningful, and real. Isn't 426 supposed to be funny and clever, not existential and real? Well, so are fourth wall breaks, I suppose.

Did that metaphor thing make sense? At least as much sense as the article? I'm trying my best here.

I dunno. I started taking this site seriously because of this. I want to write because of this. I feel different ever since reading this. Not "better" or "good", per se.

Different?

Affected?

Fulfilled.

– hide block

+1

item.title: Re: Why the additions?
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, I respect the effort. Probably just not my thing then. I have to say though, while you're here - that first time reading it was something special. Maybe that's all it is, that you can only read it for the first time once. Thanks for an incredible experience.

item.title: Re: Why the additions?
item.content:encoded: I conceived the story as a living document. The reference to an upcoming film must always be updated to a film that is not yet out, and oftentimes if there is a small element that the article would benefit from, I add it. A few occasions precluded the removal of some text for reasons I can't discuss.

item.title: Why the additions?
item.content:encoded: Question: why is this SCP still being changed and added to? I liked it originally, liked it a lot. Surreal, legitimately disturbing, hard to get through, sure, but paid off with the personal honesty and Talloran's triumph. But I came back to it today, and all the additions… I just didn't like them. It makes it even harder to get through, and I think tips it over from surreal into self-indulgent. Just because the skip was 80% incomprehensible doesn't mean it's not hurt by becoming 95% incomprehensible. Nobody's adding new diary entries to Red Reality, or creating more people to get eaten by Ananteshesha. It seems a shame to spoil such a great piece.

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded:
Brad Pitt is a celebrity, DjKaktus is not.

now listen here u little shit

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for taking the time to write this post. I appreciate the points you made here — particularly #2, but they're all good.

Stay safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3999 is the 2020 novel coronavirus pandemic

New?

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded:
Okay.

I'm coming out of permanent retirement briefly, despite the risks for my health, safety, and privacy, to note some things.

1. I'm sorry the Russian branch hates this piece and thinks of it as a joke. Understand that it's probably not a objective measure of quality and more a matter of cultural differences. The English, Chinese, French, and Japanese wikis love it because they relate to its themes. I have heard from people across the world that the article helped them understand their mental illness and helped save their life. That means that the art, no matter how cringeworthy you may imagine it is1, genuinely found an audience and, in my opinion, succeeded.

2. I'm not a "big name author." Anyone who thinks that needs some perspective because this site is so tiny on the grand scale. I wrote one popular thing and 35 others that are cult favorites. The only "big names" here are folks with substantial profiles elsewhere. Everyone else is a talented hobbyist. There is no fame to be had by desiring to be a "bigshot SCP writer." You don't feel special, you don't feel powerful, your life is most likely still a mundane struggle except bitter people and children who can't join the site yet get mad at you for clout. I know from experience the so-called "big names" hold very boring jobs and are, essentially, nobodies. We're schoolteachers, programmers, accountants, journalists, and receptionists, if we can hold down jobs at all. Stop celebritizing us, because it only leads to miserable anguish for those in this community. Brad Pitt is a celebrity, DjKaktus is not. Knock it off.

3. Yeah, the article is designed to make people feel sorry for me. That's the point of the horror here. It was written for a horror contest, and I wanted to scare people by cutting through generic creepypasta 4th wall bullshit to make it so they felt the author genuinely wasn't safe. It's not a "cry pillow" or an excuse to vent, it's fiction. It's not even about me, really. It's an artificial version of my real feelings, designed to scare you. And the fact that you actually bought, hook line and sinker, that I was actually on the verge of psychosis or whatever means the fiction even worked on you.

Now I retreat permanently into the shadows because certain hostile parties who hold negative views are dangerous to my family and my career at this point in my life.

Footnotes
1. Side note, why? Is it because people writing long dark works of art about themselves is now exclusively, cynically, the sole province of idiot emo teenagers? That is a limited blinkered view and there's genuinely good works of art out there I tried to emulate here, including many in your literary culture.

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded: That's a lot of words to express what is an apparently fundamental misunderstanding about what this article is.

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded:
Three things.

1. This is a SCP. You don't like it, yes, but it's still a SCP (a "format screw", for being more precise). And there's many stories who talk about basically the same things Stonefish talked on this article (plus, it's from several years ago and I think he's more alright. I hope, really), about mental health issues, depression, yada yada yada.

2. You imply that 3999 and 3999-like articles are objectively bad, but the only opinion you show is your own opinion. I fail to see how it is objective.

3. I'm seriously sick and tired of this type of melodramatic posts who says, just for something little, "this is soooooo horrible, you should be ashaaaaaaamed of yourself for liking it, it's the deeeaaaaaath of the Wiki". Saying that "The fact that it is so much upwoted means that we failed as a community" just because you don't like a highly-appreciated article is, frankly, ridiculous. And you exaggerate. That, and the multiple hostile jabs you throw at us just for liking this article, it's just being a huge dick, which makes this post even more ridiculous.

Apologies to everyone else if my post was not the most civil thing I've said, but I want to make it clear that making mountains out of molehills like in this post is an attitude who disrupts more than it helps.

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded: For something criticizing an article for being self-indulgent, this is pretty self-indulgent. Also melodramatic.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Preemptive reminder to everyone to please keep discussion civil in this thread, and to avoid making personal accusations.

Samarin Vladislav, if you believe that site rules have been broken by the author of this article or by voters, please state straightforwardly which rules were broken and how. I'm going through your post and currently what I'm seeing is:

You can't just do things like writing pseudo SCP-related articles with purpose of actually writing about yourself and expect that it will be just fine, only because you have a mental health problem and you can't be accountable for your words and actions later. This goes against site rules. Am I wrong about this?

Rules have exceptions for some situations and unusual people, yes, agreed, but why for LordStonefish only? Who else got royal treatment on this site?

Everyone with real (or not) mental disorders(or some other serious real life problem) can start creating things like SCP-3999, every time we will be too ashamed to say "No, sorry, we can't put this on our site, this is objectively bad and does not fit Site Rules, please read How To Write An SCP and re-write this" and quality of articles will drop. Hard.

…which seems like the implication is that the author broke site rules by writing an SCP article that does not fit your expectations for an SCP article, and also broke site rules by being upfront about the background behind the writing process for this piece. With regards to the concern about voters breaking rules by voting on the piece based on something other than the quality of the article itself, can you provide a list of comments that indicate clearly that voters did not like the piece at all but still upvoted, or upvoted solely because they felt sorry for the author and not for anything related to the piece itself?

Also, as a general note, site authors can and have been writing material similar in tone and themes to 3999, and plenty of that material ends up deleted if the audience doesn't enjoy it. All standard deletions are recorded in the Announcements forum and Announcements Archive. Also, if memory serves, the "How to Write an SCP" does not note anything about not being allowed to write material like 3999. What new guidelines are you seeking to have implemented here?

item.title: Re: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded: You're entitled to your opinion, but Jesus, this is unnecessary and hostile.

item.title: What a goddamn disaster on so many levels SCP-3999 is.
item.content:encoded:
This is not an SCP. Therefore - easiest -1 in my life.
Author, get help from professionals in real life, this isn't the place for it, really.
Dearest mobile users, I apologise for not collapsing this long post at first.
tl;dr - I read SCP-3999 and hope never to return to it ever again.

+ show block
– hide block

The fact that it is so much upwoted means that we failed as a community. I'm not exagerrating.
Here's hope one day sensitive people will stop falling for shameless attention seeking and keep SCP standarts a bit higher than this. My sincerest condolences to all people who read 3999 until the very end and felt like it's a total, headache-inducing waste of their time, because for me too it most definitely is.

I'm aware that this is sure to be an unpopular opinion here, many will probably take offence, but I had to say it, even if it will be deleted, censored or ridiculed because of my undoubtedly bad English and my "aggressiveness" towards all people, who try to use SCP Foundation for brazen elevating of their personal problems. Let me be clear about this - I wish author well and full recovery from whatever happened in his life, I don't know him, honestly don't want to know him and I don't wish to offend him personally. But the most important thing was and still is - this is not an SCP object. It is autobiographic "poor me" tale. If you can somehow miraculously convince me that it is SCP - than it's excruciatingly badly written one, which, once again, deserves a downwote for wasting readers time. Author himself agrees with this opinion, citation: "This is barely an SCP and should probably be downvoted for a number of reasons" - truer words have never been spoken about SCP 3999. There are some very long but captivating, well-written, greatly done SCP objects on this site and this is absolutely would not be one of them.

In Russian branch we already have a running gag about "researcher Talloran"(and "Ispod", local RU- branch meme, SCP-1303-RU, which currently finally re-written to readable object from the dark place beyond good and evil where it was from the first iteration) when somebody writes a very long, meaningless, borderline insane SCP. This isn't right to ruthlessly mock someone's work or state of mind and we don't do that, but since SCP-3999 was practically begging for -J article and received it(and it's a good thing about LordStonefish that he had given permission, because self-irony and humbleness is a great quality for any human being, props to him for that), I see nothing wrong in laughing about overdramatic style and truly epic size proportions of SCP-3999(once again, not about issue of mental health of its creator, that's 100% not a laughing matter). I laughed my bottom off with amazing SCP-3999-J, which is the only good thing that I got from all of this mess.
Needless fact for your pleasure: our translated SCP-3999-J in RU- branch has 7 times more upwotes then translation of SCP-3999 itself. After this I feel no need in discussing the contents of article itself. It would be completely pointless.

Anyway, it has so many upvotes not because it has some deep meaning/artistic value or interesting logs, images, audio or aura of masterfully crafted mystery or new, bright idea for SCP object or it's full of really disturbing/unsettling details and pure horror. It was upvoted, because people felt sorry for LordStoneFish, who is an accomplished SCP author and has a lot of other, much better articles that actually are solid honest-to-god SCP objects, though they are usually not much more comprehensive than this one, but that's only IMHO and purely subjective.

I don't know if I need to remind all of you about this, since members of this community obviously turned a blind eye to this, but LordStonefish said that he will leave the site as an author and stay as member of Junior Staff, citation:"this will most likely be the last thing I make for the site" and yet he still writes SCP objects to this day. Which means that he loudly said his goodbyes and presented this article, pseudo "SCP object", as his last work and said himself that it's an SCP about his mental health in real life, but didn't actually left or stopped writing after that. You can argue about technicality of his words "most likely" and his mental state at the moment, but still I believe it's wrong to use SCP Foundation site and community as your cry pillow at your excuse whenever you wish, mental disorder or not. Rules of site must be strictly obeyed by everyone and it's community must be respected by fellow members and authors. You can't just do things like writing pseudo SCP-related articles with purpose of actually writing about yourself and expect that it will be just fine, only because you have a mental health problem and you can't be accountable for your words and actions later. This goes against site rules. Am I wrong about this?

Rules have exceptions for some situations and unusual people, yes, agreed, but why for LordStonefish only? Who else got royal treatment on this site? I don't know anyone else who got away with this, to be honest. If we create "Make-A-Wish" SCP Foundation, I will have both of my hands raised in support of articles made by children with all kinds of disorders to help them feel themselves an SCP Foundation author even for a moment, even if they write basically about their own life and it has little to nothing to do about SCP. But if a grown man/woman uses Foundation to do charity for himself by creating "barely SCP objects", in author's own words, "probably his last work here", grabbing some easily generated attention on mental health issues and feeling so much better about themselves after that stunt to return shortly and keep writing - this is really bad for everyone. Everyone with real (or not) mental disorders(or some other serious real life problem) can start creating things like SCP-3999, every time we will be too ashamed to say "No, sorry, we can't put this on our site, this is objectively bad and does not fit Site Rules, please read How To Write An SCP and re-write this" and quality of articles will drop. Hard. The worst thing in all of this is that we won't be able to reverse this, since we just did an exception for LordStonefish, so why not for everyone else then? It already happened once in 2017 with SCP-3999 and also from upvotes on that object we can believe that most people seem to enjoy the article for many different reasons, but mostly - people feel sorry and they want to provide care and support for leaving and struggling author with upwote, which is totally okay with Site Rules, but actually is some very crafty manipulation of community, because author didn't actually left and our very creative and amazing authors, both new and veterans, should be strongly discouraged to ever repeat this little trick. Write with talent and popularity will come itself. I honestly believe that all of these upwotes are a massive lapse in judgement of community and we have created a horrible precedent here, first and hopefully the last one.

You deside if I'm too harsh or not, I said what I think. If I'm correct in my fears about current state of community, then I will be bombarded with accusations that I despise people with mental disorders, envy LordStonefish regalia on this site, wish to spew my hateful and harmful opinions just to troll or attack someone, etc, but I can assure you that it's not the point. My point was and still is - this is not an SCP object.

Also, a sidenote to all future authors - that "Apollyon" class is a sick joke. It exists on this site only for purpose of trying to make article look "unique"(like in this one) or to try to scare reader "ooooh, it's super-keter, boo!". Please refrain from using it, it really is unnesessary, unless you seek attention for the sake of it. Making 20-30 new classes in desperate attempts to freshen up your work completely demolishes good old "The Locked Box Test". Use Keter or any other established class instead, I beg you. This is embarassing.


Please collapse long posts as a courtesy to our mobile users. —RockTeethMothEyes

item.title: Organized Chaos
item.content:encoded: At first I thought this article was just a bizzare incoherent jumble of chaos, but as the story of researcher Talloran began to unfold within the cracks I got hooked. The pain this guy has been through, and the story it tells of the author as well. It's perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very personal article, one that makes me shudder after reading your discussion post.

It's a shame that this will be your last big article, but I will not be disappointed. I will be happy that you are making decisions that assist in your pursuit for happiness.

I hope you feel better, LordStonefish. If you need to talk with anyone, I'm always open.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A classic im only discovering now. And i loved it. Most because this is REAL fear, YOUR fear. Simple madness.
+1, and if i could, +2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I reached 3/4ths of the way through and was still confused as to what was happening. It was only when I reached the interview between Talloran and SCP-3999 that it fully solidified that it was a reality-bending/multiverse story. It is always a very intriguing and unsettling question to consider with the multiverse, because imagination becomes your double-edged sword. The unknown is a terrifying thing, so thinking of off-the-wall scenarios removes a margin of fear factor because you've conceived of it as a possibility, but the fact that you've formed the thought in the first place almost invites SCP-3999 to enact it on principle. It is the very process of conditioning the self to the absurd, painful, and insane that provides the ammo for it to continue, again, almost inviting it.

Learning that this was a farewell piece to your creative contribution to the community, it took on a whole different meaning as a combination of the writing process for SCPs, a glance at a writer's personal Word document full of dead-end/cliche ideas, backtracking, rewriting, sarcastic comments in the margins, uncertainty where to take the idea, etc. All of this is simultaneously overladen with what feels like a psychic assault of all the different stories and canons that SCP has to offer a reader and aspiring writer, as well as the callous, jaded, disdainful (one would say bordering on sneering) tone characterizing a great amount of feedback to look forward to. The desire to write something that would make the most jaded and experienced sci-fi/horror aficionado on this site, to earn a +1 rather than a -1, to push yourself to impress those who believe themselves incapable of being impressed and posture themselves as such..it can drive a person mad in the desire to earn and maintain that respect. And with that comes the existential crisis and disassociation, where you look back and think "What have I done to be such a person that I cannot measure up? Is there a world state where I succeed at this flawlessly? Is that world state the one I am currently living in? Is it in fact attainable here?

This work speaks to my desire to attain perfection and my deep-rooted belief that my worth can only be measured by my skill level, and online communities arguably exacerbate those kinds of insecurities in all of us. All in all, I'd definitely give this a +1 without question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I noticed at this paragraph, the dog is both the son, and himself (but rather than saying it is himself, like the agent was depicted as being himself later on, its just "playing the dog) and I am unsure if this is deliberate or not. I'd like to think it is, given the context of the entire written work.

The dog dresses himself in a top hat wearing a sign that says "Talent Agent" The dog dresses as the son and walks up to him and says "We have a really amazing act. You should represent us."
The agent says, "Sorry, I don't represent family acts. They're a little too cute."
The dog (playing the dog) says, "Sir, if you just see our act, we know you would want to represent us."

Secondly, are you better now? I am sorry if this has been answered before, but after reading this I was truly scared. Not from the substance, but from the complete brokenness of your psyche, and my mind itches to know if things have improved. It's been quite some years, so I figure things had?

item.title: Apollyon
item.content:encoded: Apollyon is my personal favorite SCP Object Class even though it's not officially in the Object Class list ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/object-classes]). When ever i read Apollyon Class SCPs, I just get invested in the story and the SCP. I hope one day, Apollyon will be in the official SCP Object Class list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This scip is what happens when you push insanity to its limits. It's funny because intentionally trying to make something incoherent while sticking your audience around is way harder than it sounds. For something to be interpreted as insane, sanity must first be laid out. And this article just derails at random intervals so uninvitingly, it makes you feel like stepping on firm ground only to discover that the ground was a donut shaped Earth that is violently spinning, launching you back into uneasiness.

This scip is literally a human psyche of the author. It cannot work without the writer understanding what it feels to be like Talloran, to be lost in utter confusion. The sheer volume of the article speaks for itself; you just can't write this without being experiencing it yourself; that would be like forcing a blind man to describe how a painting looks. Of course, all of this is transmitted from the author's words to the readers, like when Talloran says he went through 100 million years of torture, you can feel that million years of torture from scrolling through all those texts of meaning and meaninglessness.

This is the movie Elle, a Picasso painting of SCP. By no means is this a conventional artwork. It is completely unique in what is possesses, and that's what makes this special.

Anyway, I'm going to do my homework now.

item.title: A Rookie Watching a Veteran
item.content:encoded:
As a rookie author trying to git gud at writing articles by reading a few, this one really threw me in for a loop. All I hope is that whatever let you mean so much to this community can stay through someone else. Maybe me, maybe some other rookie, maybe some other veteran. Because this was pure magic. Pure, unadulterated, high quality magic that kept me wondering every second.

I wish I could write like that. I wish I could be a part of this magic.

Honestly, I don't know how to write this, or even what I'm writing, so I'll just finish it:
Thank you, even if I never knew you.
I hope to be able to captivate a reader the way you did me.
May this magic not die with you, or any one of us.

+2
1 for the article, 1 for the magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hwangus

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hwah hwah

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just when I thought this thing couldn't get any weirder I found the crap in the page source. What the heck?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is late, but this just means the woven metal in the walls has a hole in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check the page source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I realize I'm in an extreme minority here, but I find it frustrating that this is so highly rated. having struggled with mental illness for most of my life, I sincerely feel for the author. I actually love meta, j love fourth wall breaks, but I think there are better and more intriguing ways to do both. half of the text was just walls of strikethroughs. some of the stream of consciousness vibe was cool, but….meh. I get what this article is doing… I just don't think it does it very well. it's a pretty big wall of text with, imo, a pretty unsatisfying payoff.

can i also just say that I find it a little irritating that some of the "left wing politics" that members of the site "fight over like children" led to a SCP being maliciously brigaded, a huge rift opened up, a whole new site (that is a bald faced SCP ripoff but that's another story) created, its author repeatedly accused of creating a self-insert because one character had a few rather superficial things in common with the author, regardless of how many times they explain that its not a self insert… but this guy can write a blatant self-insert, even outright admitting it's a self-insert, one that's mostly barely coherent rambling, and it ends up one of the highest rated articles on the site? just wild. but that's NOT my reason for downvoting. I just don't like this much, I think its overrated.

also, please stop insulting people's intelligence just because they dont like a piece you like. I'm so tired of reading "well you must just have poor comprehension" whenever someone comments that they dont like something. guess what, someone disliking something you enjoy doesn't mean that they just "don't get it." I get it… I just don't much care for it.

that said, I hope the author is doing much better nowadays. and this is one of the most beloved SCPs on the site, so my opinion is clearly worth what you paid for it. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this may be my favorite read here… I've never been quite as captivated with an SCP as this one. It had me hooked from beginning to end. The mystery, the horror, the sadness, all of it executed so perfectly. Absolute beautiful tragedy. +1, but if I could I'd give it so many more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorite skips on the site period. I can't say anything about it that's already been said.

I do have to ask why this is tagged with Draven Kondraki however. I know Draven and Talloran are in some tales together but Draven isn't in this SCP at all. Not even mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a way to go out. Cheers to you, LordStonefish. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am impressed

I am

I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel numb, and I heard voices in my feckin head when reading that, it was lit af

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not much to say that hasn't already been said, but I think I just have to put it out there. Of this entire website, this is the post I keep coming back to. It's haunting, disturbing, deeply personal, profoundly disjointed and expertly crafted. It's probably the best thing on the wiki.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how I'm supposed to react to this scip.

I love it but it brings out a bunch of emotions in me.

I guess it reminds me of two scips I tried writing. Took me a while to make em, too, but I ended up being illogical and coldposting. I wonder what had convinced me to throw away all that progress, and what convinced me to abandon the ideas altogether.

So here I am, some random smiley-face pfp member, commenting something completely unrelated, on a thread whose poster literally said he was going inactive two years ago and probably stopped checking the comments 300 upvotes ago, saying a bunch of submerged thoughts that I was unaware of until I read this, when I'm supposed to be doing something productive.

+1 for the sheer audacity of the idea, the absolutely ridiculous format, and the fact that it oddly resonated with me more than any other SCP on this site in all my time as a lurker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi there.

This is a genuine question and I mean no harm or disrespect or something.

For me it's just one big disrespect for time of the reader. I don't find it original or somehow interesting.

How is this a "big disrespect for time of the reader"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am so confused that the object has so many upvotes. For me it's just one big disrespect for time of the reader. I don't find it original or somehow interesting. Downvote from me.

item.title: Well, there goes my good night’s sleep
item.content:encoded: I guess it says more about me than the article that 3999 is one of the few articles on here that has really got me shook. But +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing at all.

item.title: Re: Am I supposed to read this?
item.content:encoded: No, you're actually not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because anyone can post anything, and if it is upvoted, it stays.

The author didn't even seemed to believe this would stay, so I see no reason to berate them for posting the article. Depression is a far more important issue than a bad article, and if posting this did them any good, that's awesome.

And if this is upvoted, well. Nothing either of us can do. A lot of people here like this kind of stuff all by itself, so that's fair.

item.title: Masterpiece
item.content:encoded:
It's simply without equal. The way this transmits the reality end and the dream logic, the different stances on brainstorming and the contrast that emerges from that.

By far my favorite SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i am not good at English, but I know that if they want to do what you said, they will need more than just 5 people :)))

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so, you guys see, my native language is not English so… can you guys please tell me what "is the on ly break in the para-aramid weave" mean? please?

item.title: Am I supposed to read this?
item.content:encoded: 9112 words, 53073 chracter, most of them boring and repetitive. Got to about 1/6th before -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ZK-Class Scenarios are scenarios where the whole of reality ceases to exist

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can I ask you guys about what is ZK Class End?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just pretense. It's not an SCP, and although it's more of a tale it wouldn't fit as one of those either.
It's the author using his depression as an excuse to post a bunch of meaningless spam on an SCP page.
There's no interesting bits about this SCP, there's no engaging bits, it's just a wall of confusing, dettering text.
The author said it himself it's barely an SCP, so why has he even posted it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the style that this SCP is written in. I was wondering if anybody could recommend a book that is written in this style?

item.title: I don't know
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really feeling it. Clearly many people like this, but I'm not so sure.

It starts out decently enough. I like the idea of some supremely powerful entity getting bored enough to just mess with one person, who triumphs in the well-written dramatic speeches you composed.

However, too much of this article just devolves into heaps of "word salad" and endless repetition. While I appreciate you taking us on your journey through the writer's creative process and your other struggles, and I'm fine with the occasional "format screw" (as they're called), I think this article itself as a standalone SCP is too overly meta (most especially the first half of you describing how you developed the idea) with an extra touch of confusing and vague. A large portion of this article has nothing connecting it whatsoever, as if you took random gibberish and hammered it into paragraphs, so only certain sections of this are really worth reading at all.

No vote, but a belated well wishes to you.

P.S. I made a few grammar edits. I think one was reverted ("Talloran frantically exists stage right"), which I agree might have been intentional, but I'm letting you know in case the rest weren't too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very horrible SCP and shall be mocked by blue ballpoint pens until a salad is eaten.

This SCP is wonderful and is should be cherished by at least 35% of the world population.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow I am confused but I couldn't stop reading this either. +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the article had far too much gibberish to have a lot of impact. It came across as just a bunch of random "wacky for the sake of wackiness" back-and-forth. While the basic idea of something like I Have No Mouth as a skip, the unnecessary layer of meta denseness just kills it. When they were rare, meta articles were bold and eye-opening. So many people are jumping on the bandwagon and making this the core of their writing that it's getting seriously annoying. If this is the first article of the type you've read, yeah, it's probably pretty fun. But after you've read a few, it just starts to be the same as a normal article only with ten times the words and the actual concept diluted into a tenth of what it could be.

So, no, I don't think this article is it's own SCP. Not because it's too weird, but because it is almost entirely reusing somebody else's concept. Aside from the actual SCP 682 entry, entries featuring more of 682 aren't SCPs, they're tales. We don't need fifty SCP 682 entries in the main list. You like 682, great, make a hub or something. And we don't need fifty self-mutilating articles either. You like them, great, make a hub or something.

Edit - This is all a real shame, because the little bit of original content stuck into the overused framework is actually very good. It could be outstanding on it's own. Less is more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like one of those format-screws, almost impossible to pull off, pulled off perfectly. +1
